news_summary: Scientists have made an exciting breakthrough by discovering a previously unknown branch of Australia’s marsupials, enhancing our understanding of biodiversity.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé une avancée majeure en découvrant une branche jusqu'alors inconnue des marsupiaux d'Australie, ce qui nous permet de mieux comprendre la biodiversité.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado un avance emocionante al descubrir una rama hasta ahora desconocida de los marsupiales australianos, lo que ha permitido ampliar nuestro conocimiento sobre la biodiversidad.

articles.title: Scientists just discovered a lost branch of Australia’s marsupials
articles.summary: Fossils from Queensland suggest a newly recognized marsupial order may have survived in Australia for around 35 million years, rewriting part of the story of how the continent's unique mammals evolved. The discovery challenges the idea that Australia's marsupials all came from a single straightforward ancestral lineage.
articles.content:
A newly identified branch of the marsupial family tree is adding a surprising twist to the history of Australia's most distinctive mammals. The finding suggests that their evolution was more complicated, and more mysterious, than scientists previously understood.

Marsupials first reached Australia more than 55 million years ago. Since then, they have spread into an astonishing range of habitats and lifestyles. Today, they include about 160 species, from High Country animals (thumb-sized possums that sleep through the winter), to desert specialists in the Red Center, including tiny moles with pink fur and no eyes that spend their lives underground.

Yet despite their success, scientists still do not fully understand how marsupials diversified across Australia. Major gaps in the fossil record have left long stretches of their early history almost invisible.

Now, in a new paper published in the Journal of Paleontology, researchers from UNSW report three newly identified species that may belong to an ancient and previously unknown order of marsupials. The discovery offers a rare look at some of the earliest stages of marsupial evolution on the continent.

"Not only is it a new order, it could also be the most ancient lineage of all Australian marsupials," says UNSW paleontologist Dr. Tim Churchill.

"It may be the early ancestor of all our marsupial carnivores."

A New Branch of the Marsupial Family Tree

The standard explanation is that marsupials reached Australia after traveling from South America through Antarctica, before the ancient supercontinent Gondwana split apart.

The broad outline is widely accepted, but the details remain uncertain. Fossils dating back about 55 million years suggest that Australian marsupials may have come from a single early lineage that later divided into the marsupial groups alive today.

Those groups are currently classified into five orders within the superorder Australidelphia, which includes all living and extinct Australian marsupials (and one South American).

Dr. Churchill is now proposing a sixth order, called Keeunamorphia. According to his analysis, this group may have persisted for around 35 million years.

Members of Keeunamorphia were probably small insect eaters weighing between (25-200 grams). They lived in the forests of what is now northern Queensland before disappearing around 15 million years ago.

That landscape looked very different from the dry, open country seen there today. At the time, the region was likely covered by wet, dense rainforest and supported the ancestors of many animals still alive in Australia.

"Around 14 million years ago is when the region starts to cool again," Dr. Churchill says.

"The dense forest disappears and becomes more open woodland, with more lakes and more grasslands."

Fossils From Riversleigh

The three Keeunamorphia species described by Dr. Churchill lived around 18 million years ago. After they died, their remains ended up in shallow cave pools, where parts of their bodies were preserved at what is now the Riversleigh World Heritage Area, one of the world's most important fossil sites.

Complete skeletons are uncommon in the fossil record, so the researchers relied on much smaller clues: teeth and pieces of jaw. From those fragments, they worked to determine where the animals fit in the marsupial family tree.

To do that, the team combined fossil evidence with genetic information from living species. This allowed them to build a phylogenetic tree, a model that maps relationships between species and estimates when different branches separated over time.

"We're essentially trying to create a tree that shows both the relationships of all the different species in the tree, while also calculating when those branches probably diverged," Dr. Churchill says.

Teeth Reveal an Evolutionary Puzzle

The analysis showed that these three species lived alongside several other marsupials that scientists had already studied. However, their teeth were unusual, and they did not seem to be closely related to the other marsupials around them.

Instead, the teeth resembled those of Djarthia murgonensis, an extinct marsupial that lived about 35 million years earlier and is often viewed as a prototype for Australian marsupials.

Dr. Churchill says this points to a distinct marsupial lineage that had not been recognized before. It may have split off early in marsupial history and then survived for millions of years while other groups evolved around it.

"Whatever these things were, they seemed to be primitive compared to other marsupials at the time, and they seem to have been doing their own thing and surviving well enough alongside them," says Dr. Churchill.

Although phylogenetic trees often point to a single early group that later gave rise to modern Australian marsupials, the fossil evidence appears less tidy.

A More Complicated Origin Story

According to Dr. Churchill, early members of Keeunamorphia may have appeared not long after the first marsupials arrived in Australia from Antarctica around 55 million years ago.

If that is correct, Keeunamorphia could represent one of the earliest marsupial orders to branch off from the main lineage. That possibility challenges the simpler version of marsupial evolution, in which one ancestral group gave rise to the full diversity of Australian marsupials.

It also raises a puzzling question. If this primitive group split off so early, how did it survive for so long while remaining relatively unchanged?

"Evolutionary history is a lot more complex than just one group leading to all of Australia's marsupials after being left behind when the continent broke off from Antarctica," says Dr. Churchill.

"It's more likely that when Australia was part of Gondwana it was swarming with all sorts of bizarre, primitive marsupial-like things, and that several of them survived and led to our modern lineages."

Hidden Diversity in the Fossil Record

Much of that early diversity may still be missing from the scientific record. A nearly 20-million-year gap in the fossil history of Australian marsupials leaves room for many lineages that have not yet been found.

Some of these ancient animals may have shared a common ancestor. Others may have come from separate lineages that were left in Australia as the continents drifted apart.

Scientists may never be able to fully reconstruct the routes that early marsupials took as they spread and evolved. But each new fossil tooth pulled from Australia's ancient deposits adds another clue, making the story of marsupial evolution more complex, and far richer.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about a previously unknown ancient marsupial order in Australia, which reshapes understanding of marsupial evolution. This breakthrough has broad significance for evolutionary biology and paleontology, providing new insights into the continent's unique biodiversity history. The article is focused and detailed, describing the discovery, its context, and implications clearly.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover a lost ancient group of Australian marsupials
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Marsupials are animals like kangaroos and possums that carry their babies in pouches. They first arrived in Australia over 55 million years ago and have since developed into many different kinds. But scientists did not fully understand how all these types evolved because of missing fossils.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of New South Wales discovered fossils in northern Queensland showing a new group of marsupials. This group, called Keeunamorphia, lived about 35 million years ago and likely survived until 15 million years ago. They were small insect-eating animals that lived in rainforests, a very different environment than today’s dry lands.

**Impact** – This discovery changes what we know about marsupial evolution in Australia. Instead of all marsupials coming from one main ancestor, it shows that at least one other ancient group lived alongside others for millions of years. It means the history of these animals is more complex and includes branches scientists had not known before.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will keep studying fossils like teeth and jaws to find more evidence of these lost marsupials and learn how they lived. This could help fill in the long gaps in the fossil record and give a clearer picture of how marsupials spread and changed over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A newly found ancient group of marsupials reveals that Australia's marsupial family tree is more diverse and complicated than previously thought.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un groupe ancien perdu de marsupiaux australiens
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les marsupiaux sont des animaux comme les kangourous et les opossums qui portent leurs petits dans une poche. Ils sont arrivés en Australie il y a plus de 55 millions d’années et ont évolué en de nombreuses espèces. Mais les scientifiques ne comprenaient pas complètement leur évolution à cause du manque de fossiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Nouvelle-Galles du Sud ont découvert des fossiles dans le nord du Queensland montrant un nouveau groupe de marsupiaux. Ce groupe, appelé Keeunamorphia, a vécu il y a environ 35 millions d’années et a probablement survécu jusqu’à il y a 15 millions d’années. C’étaient de petits animaux mangeurs d’insectes vivant dans des forêts tropicales, un environnement très différent d’aujourd’hui.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension de l’évolution des marsupiaux en Australie. Au lieu que tous les marsupiaux viennent d’un seul ancêtre commun, elle montre qu’un autre groupe ancien a cohabité avec d’autres pendant des millions d’années. Cela rend leur histoire plus complexe et révèle des branches inconnues jusqu’à présent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier des fossiles comme des dents et des mâchoires pour trouver plus de preuves de ces marsupiaux disparus et mieux comprendre leur mode de vie. Cela pourrait combler les grandes lacunes dans le registre fossile et clarifier comment les marsupiaux se sont répandus et ont évolué.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un groupe ancien de marsupiaux découvert récemment montre que l’arbre généalogique des marsupiaux australiens est plus diversifié et complexe que ce que l’on pensait.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren un antiguo grupo perdido de marsupiales australianos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los marsupiales son animales como los canguros y los zarigüeyas que llevan a sus crías en una bolsa. Llegaron a Australia hace más de 55 millones de años y se han convertido en muchas especies diferentes. Pero los científicos no entendían completamente cómo evolucionaron debido a la falta de fósiles.

**Qué sucedió** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Nueva Gales del Sur encontraron fósiles en el norte de Queensland que muestran un nuevo grupo de marsupiales. Este grupo, llamado Keeunamorphia, vivió hace unos 35 millones de años y probablemente sobrevivió hasta hace 15 millones. Eran animales pequeños que comían insectos y vivían en bosques tropicales, un ambiente muy diferente al de hoy.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia lo que sabemos sobre la evolución de los marsupiales en Australia. En lugar de que todos los marsupiales vinieran de un ancestro único, muestra que otro grupo antiguo vivió junto a otros durante millones de años. Esto hace que la historia de estos animales sea más complicada y muestre ramas que los científicos no conocían.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando fósiles como dientes y mandíbulas para encontrar más pruebas sobre estos marsupiales perdidos y entender mejor cómo vivían. Esto podría ayudar a llenar los grandes huecos en el registro fósil y dar una imagen más clara de cómo se dispersaron y cambiaron los marsupiales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un grupo antiguo recién descubierto revela que el árbol familiar de los marsupiales australianos es más diverso y complejo de lo que se pensaba.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned painting of three small, ancient marsupial silhouettes with distinctive unusual teeth shapes, nestled among lush, dense prehistoric Queensland rainforest foliage near a softly glowing shallow cave pool, with a faint, branching tree of life subtly woven into the background symbolizing the newly discovered lost marsupial lineage.
